sanskrit_devanagari:
अर्जुन उवाच
ज्यायसी चेत्कर्मणस्ते मता बुद्धिर्जनार्दन ।
तत्किं कर्मणि घोरे मां नियोजयसि केशव ॥ १ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
arjuna uvāca
jyāyasī cet karmaṇas te matā buddhir janārdana |
tat kiṁ karmaṇi ghore māṁ niyojayasi keśava || 1 ||

anvaya.meaning: O agitator-of-people (an epithet of the Teacher)

translations.verbatim_quote: Arjuna said: O Janārdana, O Keśava, why do You want to engage me in this ghastly warfare, if You think that intelligence is better than fruitive work?
Arjuna said: O Janardana, O Keshava, why do You want to engage me in this ghastly warfare, if You think that intelligence is better than fruitive work?

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt; full purport length: 1276 chars)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: The Supreme Personality of Godhead Śrī Kṛṣṇa has very elaborately described the constitution of the soul in the previous chapter, with a view to delivering His intimate friend Arjuna from the ocean of material grief.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 3.1 O Janardana, cet, if it be; te, Your; mata, opinion, intention; that buddhih, Wisdom; jyayasi, is superior; karmanah, to action-. If the combination of Wisdom and action be intended (by the Lord), then the means to Liberation is only one.
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-3-1-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Adi Shankaracharya of Advaita Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: The Advaita reading: the questioner's question rests on a misreading. If buddhi (= jñāna-niṣṭhā, knowledge-steadfastness) is intended as superior, and the Teacher also engages the questioner in karma, then a SEQUENCE has been misread as a CONTRADICTION. The chapter's argument is that knowledge and action are not alternatives but a sequence: karma-yoga purifies → jñāna-niṣṭhā arises.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 3.1 ‘Arjuna said — If you consider that knowledge is superior to works, why do you engage me in this terrible deed?’ What is said here is this: If the firm adherence to knowledge is the only means to the vision of the self, then how can one accept the idea that devotion to works (Karma) leads to it?…
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-3-1-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Ramanuja of Sri Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: The Vishishtadvaita reading: the questioner's question is the natural one for the seeker. If firm devotion to knowledge is the means to the vision of the self, and if that devotion arises only from cessation of sensory and mental activities, then why should one engage in works that are themselves activities of the senses? The chapter is the Teacher's correction of this seeker-confusion.

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: The questioner's question hinges on what 'buddhi' means here. The Advaita reading takes it specifically as jñāna-niṣṭhā (knowledge-steadfastness) — the path of cessation of activity that culminates in self-vision. The Vishishtadvaita reading takes it as 'firm devotion to knowledge' that arises after sense-cessation. The two modern translators flatten these to 'intelligence' or 'knowledge'. The question's force depends on the strong reading: if the goal is the cessation-path, why is the Teacher telling me to engage in this terrible deed?

literal_meaning: The questioner said: O addressed-one, if you consider buddhi (intelligence) superior to action, why then do you engage me in this terrible action?
traditional_meaning_consensus: 3.1 opens chapter 3 with the questioner's pushback. After hearing 2.49's ranking of buddhi-yoga above mere fruit-driven action, the questioner takes 'buddhi' as jñāna-niṣṭhā (knowledge-steadfastness) and concludes the call to action is contradictory. The verse's tension drives the entire chapter; per the Advaita reading, the questioner has misread sequence as contradiction; per the Vishishtadvaita reading, the question is the natural one for a seeker who has not yet integrated knowledge with action. Either way, this verse is the chapter's dialectical opening.
